hero.tagline: Ayurveda, TCM, Kampo, Andean, and folk traditions — side by side. Search by taste, body region, or season; compare how five cultures answer the same complaint.

home.startHereBody: Pick a concern to see remedies ranked across all five traditions, each with safety flags and modern references.
home.previewBody: A taste of what's inside — no login required.

features.compassBody: Pick warming + pungent + bitter — watch matching herbs spiral in.
features.bodyMapBody: Tap a body region to see remedies from every tradition.
features.compareBody: One concern, five answers, side by side.
features.graphBody: See which plants pair, synergise, or conflict.
features.recipeBody: Drag herbs into a pot. Live doses and interaction warnings.
features.ritualsBody: Knowledge becomes practice — with timers and ambient sound.
features.seasonalBody: What's in season, where you are, right now.
features.quizBody: Three questions to your first protocol.

faq.a1: No. Remedia is an educational encyclopedia of traditional remedies. Herbs can interact with medications and have real contraindications in pregnancy, breastfeeding, and chronic disease. Always consult a qualified practitioner before use.
faq.a2: Five: Ayurveda (India), Traditional Chinese Medicine (China), Kampo (Japan), Andean & Peruvian herbalism (including Spanish folk), and other global traditions from Europe, Africa, the Middle East, the Americas, and the Pacific.
faq.q3: How are remedies sourced and verified?
faq.a3: Each entry cites the tradition it comes from and, where available, links to authoritative modern sources like NCCIH, WHO monographs, Cochrane reviews, PubMed, and the Kew POWO taxonomy database. Entries are tagged traditional, verified, or preliminary.
faq.q4: Can I use this during pregnancy or breastfeeding?
faq.a4: Every remedy carries structured pregnancy and lactation safety flags. Many traditional herbs are contraindicated in pregnancy. Check the safety panel on each remedy and consult a licensed practitioner before use.
faq.q5: How do I find remedies for my symptom?
faq.a5: Use the body-map symptom finder, the cross-tradition compare view, the taste-and-energetics compass, or press Ctrl/⌘K anywhere to open the search palette.

compass.title: The Compass
compass.body: Every traditional medicine — Ayurveda, TCM, Kampo, Greek humourism — teaches plants first by taste and temperature. Pick the qualities you want, and the library reorganises itself around you.

bodyMap.title: Body Map
bodyMap.body: Tap a region. See the plants five traditions reach for when that part of the body is asking for help.

compare.title: Five Traditions, One Concern
compare.body: Ask any question of the library and see how Ayurveda, Andean curanderismo, TCM, Japanese Kampo, and global folk medicine each reply. This is the only page like it on the internet.

graph.title: The Herb Graph
graph.body: Concerns sit in a ring; herbs orbit toward the concerns they traditionally address. Herbs that bridge multiple concerns land in the middle — they're the generalists every tradition reaches for.

recipe.title: Recipe Builder
recipe.body: Drag herbs into the pot below. Your blend calculates traditional tea ratios in real time and flags any known herb–herb or herb–drug interactions.

rituals.title: Guided Rituals
rituals.body: A herbal book you read is information. A herbal ritual you practise is medicine. Each guide walks you through preparation with gentle timers and a quieting atmosphere.

seasonal.title: Your Apothecary Calendar
seasonal.body: Traditional medicine is seasonal medicine. Below — what your body is most likely asking for right now, and which plants are at their peak. Hemisphere detected from your timezone.

quiz.title: Find your remedy
quiz.body: Answer three quick questions to get personalized suggestions across five traditions.
quiz.disclaimer: Educational guidance only — this is not a medical assessment.

encyclopedia.title: Encyclopedia
encyclopedia.body: {count} remedies — instant search across name, Latin, benefit. Tap ⌘K anywhere for global search.
encyclopedia.locked: The full encyclopedia (with recipes, mechanisms, sourcing, safety flags, and references) requires a subscription.

symptoms.title: Browse by concern
symptoms.body: Pick a concern below and see remedies from every tradition ranked by how directly they address it, filtered down from {count} entries. Each result links into the full monograph with safety flags and references.

blurbs.Sleep & Anxiety: Settle the nervous system — valerian, ashwagandha, passionflower, tulsi, kampo yokukansan.
blurbs.Cough & Respiratory: Open airways, ease the throat — mullein, licorice, thyme, ma-huang formulas, pi-pa-gao.
blurbs.Digestion: Appetite, bloating, nausea, IBS — ginger, peppermint, hingvashtak, rikkunshitō, chamomile.
blurbs.Joints & Pain: Inflammatory and muscular — turmeric, boswellia, nirgundi oil, daruharidra, warming oils.
blurbs.Immunity & Fatigue: Adaptogens and rasayanas — ashwagandha, reishi, astragalus, schisandra, eleuthero.
blurbs.Women's Health: Menstrual, menopausal, fertility — shatavari, dong quai, tōki-shakuyaku-san, red raspberry.
blurbs.Men's Vitality: Libido, prostate, stamina — ashwagandha, safed musli, maca, saw palmetto, eucommia.
blurbs.Skin: Topical and internal — turmeric, neem, calendula, manjistha, centella.
blurbs.Heart & BP: Circulation, cholesterol, BP — hawthorn, arjuna, garlic, hibiscus, olive leaf.
blurbs.Liver & Detox: Hepatoprotective and bitter — milk thistle, bhumyamalaki, dandelion, artichoke.
blurbs.Blood Sugar: Glycemic modulators — cinnamon, gymnema, fenugreek, bitter melon, berberine.

favorites.title: Your favorites
favorites.empty: No favorites yet. Tap the heart on any remedy to save it here.

signin.syncNote: Your favorites and preferences will sync across devices.

me.title: The Hearth
me.body: A quiet record of what you've practised. Stored only in your browser — private by default.
me.heatmapHint: One square = one day. Darker = more rituals that day.

social.title: Follow Remedia
social.body: A daily rhythm of remedies, rituals, and recipes from five healing traditions. Pick the feeds that fit your life.

tiktok.lead: 60-second explainers, one remedy per day. Tap through to the full encyclopedia when you want the evidence and sourcing.

instagram.lead: Recipe carousels you can save, plant photography from source markets, and reels that go deeper than the hashtags.

methods.title: Evidence Grading Schema
methods.subtitle: How we evaluate clinical evidence for herbal remedies
methods.overview1: Every substantive benefit claim on Remedia carries an evidence grade based on the quality and quantity of clinical research. Our five-tier system lets you quickly assess whether a claim is supported by robust clinical trials or rests on traditional use alone.
methods.overview2: Grades are assigned by reviewing PubMed-indexed studies, prioritizing randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and meta-analyses. We flag conflicting evidence, small sample sizes, and lack of replication. Traditional use is explicitly marked when no modern clinical data exists.
methods.sources1: We primarily search PubMed for clinical trials, meta-analyses, and systematic reviews. Study details (design, sample size, duration, dose, primary outcome) are extracted and linked via PMID for direct verification.

sourceBullets.1: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) carry the highest weight
sourceBullets.2: Meta-analyses and systematic reviews are preferred when available
sourceBullets.3: Open-label and observational studies are downgraded to "preliminary" or "unclear"
sourceBullets.4: Animal and in-vitro studies are marked as preliminary only
sourceBullets.5: Traditional use is documented from classical texts and ethnobotanical sources

methods.contradictions1: When modern clinical evidence contradicts traditional use, we surface this explicitly. Traditional claims that have been disproven by well-designed studies are marked with a "Contradicted" badge and linked to the contradictory research.
methods.contradictions2: This approach honors both traditional knowledge and modern science — users can see what has been used for centuries while understanding what the evidence actually supports.
methods.exampleText: A herb traditionally used for "cancer treatment" would show as Traditional (○○○○○) with a contradiction badge if RCTs show no anticancer effect — allowing users to see both the classical use and the modern assessment.
methods.transparency: Every claim links to the underlying studies. Click any PMID to view the abstract on PubMed. If you find a study we missed or believe a grade is misclassified, please let us know — we update the corpus as new research appears.

grades.likelyDesc: ≥ 2 well-designed RCTs OR a meta-analysis, consistent direction, clinically meaningful effect
grades.possiblyDesc: 1 good RCT or multiple smaller / mixed trials leaning positive
grades.unclearDesc: Conflicting trials, or single small trial not replicated
grades.ineffectiveDesc: Well-designed studies show no meaningful benefit; contradicts traditional claims
grades.traditionalDesc: No modern clinical studies; classical use documented

conventions.title: Preparation & Measurement
conventions.body: Recipes in this encyclopedia use the shorthand below. Where an entry gives only a preparation type — "infusion", "decoction", "tincture" and so on — the standard dose here applies unless the entry states otherwise.
conventions.unit1Note: (varies with density — ginger powder is denser than chamomile)
conventions.unit5Rest: varies by dropper; 20 drops ≈ 1 ml is a safe default
conventions.warning: Always start at the lowest dose in the range. Children, elderly, pregnant or breastfeeding individuals, and those on medication should reduce by half or consult a qualified practitioner before use.

legal.onlyEnglish: ⚠ This legal document is currently maintained only in English. For translated summaries or questions, please contact us.

tasteCompass.exampleHint: Try <b1>bitter + cooling</b1> for a classic liver-cleansing profile, or <b2>sweet + moistening</b2> for nourishing tonics, or <b3>pungent + warming</b3> to kindle digestion.
tasteCompass.showingTop: Showing top {count} by strength of match.

compare.noRemedy: No documented remedy in this tradition for "{concern}" yet.
compare.footerNote: Note: Traditions have different theories of illness — "digestion" in Ayurveda (agni) is not identical to "digestion" in TCM (Spleen/Stomach). This view shows what each tradition commonly reaches for, not a claim that the remedies treat the same underlying mechanism.

bodyMap.showingOne: Showing {shown} of {total} remedy across all traditions.
bodyMap.showingMany: Showing {shown} of {total} remedies across all traditions.
bodyMap.noMatches: No matches yet for this region — our mapping is conservative.

seasonal.thisMonthAsks: This month the body asks for

graph.concernPillsDesc: The 11 large pills arranged in a ring are body-system concerns. Every herb is pulled toward the concerns it traditionally addresses.
graph.dotsHerbsDesc: Dot colour = region of origin. Dot size = number of connections (larger dots bridge more concerns). Drag a dot to rearrange; click it for details.
graph.linesRelationsDesc: Toggle Concern / Taste / Energetic to switch what the lines mean. "Concern" anchors herbs to their clusters; the other two reveal cross-tradition taste and temperature affinity.
graph.focusConcernHint: Focus the graph on this concern to see only the herbs traditions reach for when it arises.

quiz.chronicQ: How long has this been going on?
quiz.preferenceQ: Which tradition are you most drawn to?

recipe.empty: Empty — add ingredients from the left.
recipe.dropHere: Drop herbs here to begin · you can reorder by dragging
recipe.methodText: Combine. Steep in just-boiled water (~95 °C) for 8–10 min. Strain. Drink warm.
recipe.exportMethod: Method: Steep combined herbs in just-boiled water (~95°C) for 8–10 min. Strain. Drink warm.

medications.label: Medications I take
medications.searchPlaceholder: Search drug class or brand (e.g. warfarin, prozac, ibuprofen…)
medications.noMatch: No match — the drug class may not be covered yet.
medications.trustNote: Stored in this browser only. Not sent anywhere. This is an OTC-level screen, not a substitute for your pharmacist.
